projects:
ARSÈNE MANZI — COMPLETE CANDIDATE CONTEXT DOCUMENT
For AI Interview Assistant (RAG Knowledge Base)
Last Updated: June 2026

SECTION 1: WHO IS ARSÈNE MANZI
Full Name: Arsène Manzi
Location: Edmond, Oklahoma, United States
LinkedIn: linkedin.com/in/arsène-manzi-98a6a0178
GitHub: github.com/lacebx
Twitter/X: @0xC_137
Status: Computer Science student at Oklahoma Christian University, currently open to work (on-site, hybrid, or remote)
Languages: English and French (bilingual)
Network: 700+ LinkedIn followers, 500+ connections
Professional Identity: Arsène is a builder-first engineer who identifies as an AI Developer, backend engineer, and emerging tech enthusiast. He has shipped multiple real applications — from a blockchain-based national digital currency system to a RAG-powered AI chatbot to a live marketplace platform — while still completing his degree. He works under the EpiqueAI GitHub organization for collaborative projects and under his personal lacebx handle for personal and experimental projects.
Core Personality in Interviews: Arsène is calm, conversational, and honest about gaps. He does not bluff. He names what he knows deeply, names what he's ramping on, and always connects abstract concepts back to something he has literally built.

SECTION 2: EDUCATION
Institution: Oklahoma Christian University
Degree: Bachelor of Science in Computer Science
Location: Edmond, Oklahoma
Status: Currently enrolled (expected graduation near 2025–2026)
Notable: Arsène built multiple production-level projects while enrolled — not toy projects, but deployed systems with real users.

SECTION 3: AFFILIATIONS & EXPERIENCE
RadicalX
RadicalX is a project-based professional fellowship platform. Arsène has worked through RadicalX on structured team engineering challenges. This is listed on his LinkedIn as an active affiliation.
EpiqueAI
EpiqueAI is a GitHub organization Arsène is part of. The CBDC Rwanda system (four repositories — frontend, backend, admin-frontend, admin-backend) was built under this organization. This represents his most significant team engineering work and involves real-world fintech infrastructure including blockchain integration.

SECTION 4: PROJECT — CBDC RWANDA WALLET SYSTEM (EpiqueAI)
Overview
The Rwanda CBDC (Central Bank Digital Currency) Wallet System is a full-stack fintech application simulating a national digital currency infrastructure. It is split into four repositories: a user-facing frontend, a user backend, an admin frontend, and an admin backend. Arsène's GitHub handle (lacebx) appears as the committer across all four repos.
This is Arsène's most technically comprehensive team project. It involves:
Real blockchain interactions on the Ethereum Sepolia testnet
Smart contract deployment using Hardhat
Server-side wallet key management
JWT authentication across multiple services
Separation of concerns between user-facing and admin-facing backends

4a. CBDC User Frontend (EpiqueAI/cbdc-frontend)
What it is: The citizen-facing web application for the Rwanda CBDC wallet.
Tech Stack:
TypeScript (95.9% of codebase)
Vite (build tool)
Tailwind CSS
Supabase integration (in /supabase directory)
Dockerized
Features:
User registration and login
Automatic wallet creation on signup
View wallet balance (pulled from the blockchain)
Send funds using email or phone number (not wallet addresses — abstracted for UX)
View transaction history (sent and received)
Notifications for incoming transactions
Responsive, modern UI
Architecture: Decoupled from all blockchain operations. The frontend is purely a UI layer — it talks to the backend API, which handles all Ethereum interactions. This was a deliberate security decision.
Key Design Decision: Users send money using email or phone numbers instead of wallet addresses. This user-friendly abstraction was built into the backend routing logic.

4b. CBDC User Backend (EpiqueAI/cbdc-backend)
What it is: The main backend service for the Rwanda CBDC system. This is the most technically complex piece of the entire system.
Tech Stack:
Node.js (v16+)
Express.js
MySQL (shared database with admin backend)
ethers.js (for wallet generation and blockchain interaction)
Hardhat (for smart contract compilation and deployment)
Ethereum Sepolia testnet
Docker
Project Structure:
app.js — Main Express application
routes/ — Route definitions
controllers/ — Business logic
middleware/ — Authentication and request handling
db.js — MySQL database connection
artifacts/ — Compiled smart contract artifacts
cbdc-contracts/ — Hardhat workspace for smart contracts
Core Features:
User registration with automatic wallet creation
Wallet address and private key generation via ethers.js
All private keys stored server-side in MySQL (never exposed to client)
Balance fetching directly from the Sepolia blockchain
Fund transfer by email or phone (backend resolves to wallet address internally)
Transaction signing done entirely server-side
Transaction history retrieval
Notifications for incoming transactions
Admin-triggered user blocking
Smart Contract — WarrantyNFT:
There is a WarrantyNFT smart contract in the Hardhat workspace
It is minted when a user completes a simulated purchase in a shop feature
Users can view their NFTs under a "My Warranties" section with metadata and Etherscan links
Deployment steps: install dependencies in cbdc-contracts/, set RPC_URL and PRIVATE_KEY env vars, run npm run build then npm run deploy, which exports the ABI and contract address to the backend
API Endpoints:
POST /api/users/register — Register user, auto-create wallet
POST /api/users/login — Login, receive JWT
Additional endpoints for balance, transactions, and notifications
ngrok: Arsène added ngrok support to expose the local backend during development and testing.
Security Philosophy: All blockchain operations happen on the server. Private keys never leave the server. Admin operations are handled by a completely separate service (admin backend).

4c. CBDC Admin Frontend (EpiqueAI/cbdc-admin-frontend)
What it is: The government administrator dashboard — a separate, secured interface for managing the CBDC system.
Tech Stack:
TypeScript (94% of codebase)
React (Create React App)
Tailwind CSS
JWT-based authentication for all admin API calls
Dockerized
Features:
Secure admin login (JWT)
View all registered users
Block or unblock user accounts
View transaction history for any user
Privileged actions completely separate from user-facing functions
Architecture: This frontend is a separate application from the user frontend, hitting a separate backend. The separation ensures admin capabilities are never exposed in the user-facing codebase.

4d. CBDC Admin Backend (EpiqueAI/cbdc-admin-backend)
What it is: The separate backend service for government administrators only.
Tech Stack:
Node.js (v16+)
Express.js
MySQL (shared database with user backend)
JWT middleware protecting all routes
Docker
Project Structure:
index.js — Entry point
routes/ — Admin route definitions
middleware/ — JWT verification middleware
db.js — Database connection
Admin-Only Endpoints:
List all users
Block a user
Unblock a user
View all transactions for any given user
Security: Every single endpoint is protected by JWT middleware. The JWT_SECRET environment variable must match the one used in the user backend. Database is shared with the main backend (same MySQL instance) but access is restricted to privileged routes.

How to Talk About the CBDC System in an Interview
"This was a full national-scale digital currency system built across four repos — user frontend, user backend, admin frontend, admin backend."
"I handled backend signing of all transactions so users never touched private keys."
"The send-by-email/phone feature required backend address resolution — we stored the wallet address against user accounts and did the lookup internally."
"The WarrantyNFT contract was deployed on Sepolia via Hardhat — I set up the compile-deploy pipeline that exports the ABI and address to the backend."
"Admin and user operations are completely separated at the service level — different backends, different authentication contexts, different frontends."

SECTION 5: PROJECT — NEEDL (lacebx/needl-main)
Overview
Needl is an AI-driven peer-to-peer marketplace for second-hand and specialty goods. It's Arsène's most feature-complete product-style backend project. Sellers photograph products; the AI analyzes photos to verify authenticity, assess condition, generate descriptions, and suggest fair market prices automatically.
GitHub: github.com/lacebx/needl-main
Status: Private repo, ~2 years old, README updated 4 months ago

Tech Stack
Frontend: React 18.2.0, Material-UI, React Router, React-Konva (canvas)
Backend: Node.js, Express.js, MongoDB, Sharp (image processing), Multer (file uploads)
AI: OpenAI API
Auth: JWT, Bcrypt
Payments: Stripe API
Delivery: Uber API (real-time delivery quotes)
Infrastructure: Docker

Backend Server Structure
index.js — Express server entry point
agent.quality.js — AI quality verification agent
generator.js — AI listing description generator
items.js — Product listing routes
auth.js — Authentication routes (JWT + bcrypt)
middleware/ — Auth middleware

AI Agents (This is Critical to Discuss)
Quality Verification Agent (agent.quality.js):
Uses OpenAI API and custom ML analysis
Assesses product authenticity (is this item genuinely what it claims to be?)
Evaluates condition (excellent, good, fair, poor)
Estimates market value
Returns a structured JSON result — not free-form text — so downstream logic can act on it
If the image is too blurry or unidentifiable, it returns a structured error with a reason, not a silent failure
Generator Agent (generator.js):
Takes the output from the quality agent plus the image analysis
Synthesizes a professional product listing description automatically
Uses market insights and condition data to create compelling, accurate copy

Image Pipeline
Multer handles file upload (multipart form data)
Sharp normalizes, resizes, and compresses images before they go to AI
This preprocessing step was important — it prevented bad-quality inputs from reaching the OpenAI API and wasting tokens

Pricing Engine
Calculates fair market price range using 50+ criteria
Considers: product condition, market trends, comparable listings, historical pricing data, demand indicators
Returns min/max price suggestion to the seller

Other Features
Inventory management for sellers
Bulk upload capability
Sales and market trend analytics dashboard
Stripe integration for payments and seller payouts
Uber API for real-time delivery quote generation

How to Talk About Needl in an Interview
"Needl is where I first built production AI agents with real structured contracts — the quality agent returns a typed JSON object, not just text, so the generator can act on it deterministically."
"The image preprocessing pipeline was critical — Sharp normalizes everything before it hits OpenAI. Bad input kills the whole thing otherwise."
"I built both the AI layer and the full backend — auth, routes, payments, third-party integrations."
"The pricing engine doesn't just call an LLM — it runs a structured evaluation across multiple market signals."

SECTION 6: PROJECT — ASYNC AI WORKFLOW LAB (lacebx/async-ai-workflow-lab)
Overview
A hands-on engineering lab Arsène built to understand and demonstrate production-style async AI workflow patterns. It is a playground with a real working system — FastAPI, Celery, Redis, PostgreSQL — designed to simulate how financial document workflows should be built and to practice system-design interview answers.
GitHub: github.com/lacebx/async-ai-workflow-lab
Language: JavaScript/Python
Status: Public repo, updated ~1 month ago

Tech Stack
API Layer: FastAPI (Python) — stateless, validates input, writes to Postgres, enqueues to Celery, returns 202 immediately
Message Broker: Redis — decouples the API from the workers
Worker: Celery — picks up jobs, executes the workflow orchestrator
Database: PostgreSQL — stores workflow state, per-step outputs, logs, checkpoints
UI: Static HTML/CSS/JS — monitoring dashboard and learning lab
Infrastructure: Docker + Docker Compose (single command to spin up everything)

How the Pipeline Works
User submits a request via FastAPI endpoint
FastAPI validates, writes a pending record to PostgreSQL with a job ID
FastAPI enqueues the job to Redis and returns HTTP 202 immediately
Celery worker picks up the job from Redis
Worker executes fin.run_workflow — the orchestrator
Orchestrator runs steps in order: research_agent → summary_agent
Each step writes its output to PostgreSQL before passing control (checkpoint)
If a step fails, it retries from that step — not from the beginning
UI allows monitoring, tracing, and failure simulation

Agent Handoff Contract
Agents communicate using a JSON handoff contract — structured typed data passed between steps. This is deliberately not free-form text. The contract is inspectable, loggable, and testable. Arsène chose this pattern specifically to keep the pipeline debuggable.

Failure Scenarios the Lab Covers
The learning lab UI includes interactive failure scenario tabs:
Redis is down — what happens to the queue?
Duplicate message delivery — how do idempotent handlers protect you?
Invalid handoffs between agents — what happens when the contract breaks?
Retry storms — how do you prevent a failing job from hammering the system?

Key Concepts Demonstrated
Idempotency: Handlers can be re-run without side effects
Checkpoints: Per-step output written to Postgres before proceeding
Replay: Because outputs are saved, you can resume from any step
202 Pattern: API never blocks; always returns immediately, client polls for status
Decoupling: API and workers are completely separate processes

How to Talk About This in an Interview
"I built this lab specifically to understand the plumbing of async AI workflows — not to ship a product, but to be able to break things deliberately and understand why."
"The FastAPI layer never does heavy work. It validates, writes to Postgres, enqueues, and returns 202. The worker handles everything else."
"The JSON handoff contract between agents is the most important design decision — it makes every step debuggable because you can see exactly what went in and what came out."
"I can spin up the whole system with one Docker Compose command and run failure scenarios against it."

SECTION 7: PROJECT — ME0 MINI MVP (lacebx/me0-mini_mvp)
Overview
Me0 is Arsène's personal virtual assistant chatbot. It answers questions about his background, skills, and projects in first-person. It is built entirely from scratch using a RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) architecture — no off-the-shelf chatbot framework. It was deployed and served as a live widget on his portfolio site.
GitHub: github.com/lacebx/me0-mini_mvp
Language: Python
Status: Public repo, 11 months old, deployed on Railway

Tech Stack
Core AI: GPT-Neo (generative model), sentence-transformers (for embeddings), FAISS (vector similarity search)
ML Libraries: transformers, torch, scikit-learn, numpy
Document Processing: PyPDF2, pdfplumber, python-docx
API Layer: Flask, Flask-Cors, Gunicorn
Deployment: Railway (auto-redeploys on Git push), Procfile-based
Knowledge Base: curated_data.json



SECTION 8: PROJECT — OPENKORA CONNECT (lacebx/openkora-connect)
Overview
OpenKora Connect is an open-source community platform built to connect developers, students, and companies around open-source innovation. Described as "GitHub + Dev.to + Product Hunt + LinkedIn," it serves as a hub for building, showcasing, and supporting open-source projects. Arsène built and leads this platform with a vision of becoming the home of open-source innovation, starting from Rwanda. The program allows for organizations to create accounts and sponsor developers, furthermore, recruiters can also create accounts and scout for developers. 

GitHub: github.com/lacebx/openkora-connect
Language: TypeScript / JavaScript
Status: Private repo, live on Vercel (openkora-connect.vercel.app), 42 commits, 6 branches, 34 deployments

Tech Stack
Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, Vite
UI/Styling: shadcn/ui, Tailwind CSS
Backend: Supabase (PostgreSQL, Auth, Storage, Edge Functions)
State Management: React Query (TanStack Query)
Routing: React Router
Deployment: Vercel

Key Concepts
Full-Stack Architecture with BaaS: Demonstrates the ability to build a production-grade application using Supabase as a full backend — handling auth, database, storage, and serverless edge functions without a separate server.
Component-Driven UI with shadcn/ui: Leverages a modern, accessible component library with Tailwind CSS to build a consistent, responsive interface rapidly.
AI-Augmented Features: Integrates AI for personalized project recommendations and collaborator matching, demonstrating applied ML beyond model training — specifically recommendation system logic.
Real-World Collaboration Workflows: Implements contributor management, join requests, and a messaging system — reflecting the workflows of actual open-source platforms.
Incentive Systems: Badge and sponsorship tracking systems show product thinking beyond core CRUD functionality.

Failure Scenarios
Supabase Vendor Lock-In: The platform is heavily dependent on Supabase for auth, DB, and storage. Migrating away would require significant refactoring — a trade-off made deliberately for speed of development.
Real-Time Scalability: The current messaging system does not use WebSockets natively. At scale, this could introduce latency. A future improvement would be Supabase Realtime channels.
AI Recommendation Cold Start: The AI-powered project recommendations and collaborator matching require sufficient user data to be meaningful. A new platform with few users would surface low-quality suggestions.
Content Moderation: With user-generated projects and articles, there is no automated moderation pipeline yet — a known gap for platform safety at scale.

How to Talk About This in an Interview
"OpenKora Connect is my flagship full-stack project. I chose to build the platform I wish existed — one that connects African developers and students to real open-source opportunities and each other."
"The core architecture challenge was using Supabase as the sole backend layer. That meant designing the entire data model in PostgreSQL, managing row-level security policies as this program is multi tenant, and writing Edge Functions for server-side logic — all without a traditional Express or Django backend."
"I integrated AI features for project recommendations and collaborator matching. This required thinking about feature engineering — what signals from a user profile actually predict a good project match — which is a real applied ML problem separate from model training."
"One thing I'm proud of is the contributor workflow: join requests, approval flows, and messaging between collaborators. That's not just CRUD — it reflects how real open-source contribution actually works."
readme: \# BPE Tokenizer in Rust Rust port of \[minbpe](https://github.com/karpathy/minbpe) with a small CLI and Docker image for train / encode / decode. The point was reference parity, not a faster tokenizer. Supports: \- `BasicTokenizer` , byte-level BPE on raw UTF-8 \- `RegexTokenizer` , GPT-4 style regex chunks, then same BPE logic per chunk \- JSON model files matching the assessment format \- Line-by-line encode/decode (splits on `\\n` only) \## Development notes Determinism took more time than expected. Merge ties and encode-time merge ranking both had to match minbpe exactly , reference JSON under `data/reference/` was the source of truth. Correctness came before speed. Training re-scans pairs each step; that's fine for this project size. Validation scripts (`tests/submission\_val.sh`, `cargo test`) were added early so merge drift didn't slip through silently. AI tools were used for scaffolding and docs; see `AGENTS.md`. Algorithm choices were checked manually against reference outputs. \## Algorithms \### Basic BPE Start from bytes 0–255, learn merges until `vocab\_size`, encode by repeatedly applying the lowest-rank available merge (minbpe style). \### Regex BPE Split text with the GPT-4 pattern (via `fancy-regex`), train/encode each chunk separately, concatenate token ids. Merges never cross chunk boundaries. Pattern stored in the model JSON: ```text '(?i:\[sdmt]|ll|ve|re)|\[^\\r\\n\\p{L}\\p{N}]?+\\p{L}+|\\p{N}{1,3}| ?\[^\\s\\p{L}\\p{N}]++\[\\r\\n]\*|\\s\*\[\\r\\n]|\\s+(?!\\S)|\\s+ ``` \## Project layout ```text . ├── AGENTS.md # agent instructions (assessment) ├── Dockerfile ├── Cargo.toml ├── README.md ├── docs/ │ ├── PROCESS.md # dev log │ └── logo.png ├── src/ │ ├── main.rs │ └── tokenizer/ ├── tests/ │ └── submission\_val.sh └── data/ ├── train.txt ├── test.txt └── reference/ # expected models + token outputs ``` \## Build ```bash docker build -t bpe . ``` Pinned builder image: `rust:1.86-bookworm` (see `Dockerfile`). For a fully clean rebuild: ```bash docker build --no-cache -t bpe . ``` Local (no Docker): ```bash cargo build --release ``` \## CLI help ```bash docker run --rm bpe --help ``` Example output: ```text Byte Pair Encoding tokenizer CLI Usage: bpe Commands: train Train a tokenizer model from a corpus encode Encode text into token ID arrays (one array per input line) decode Decode token ID arrays back into text (one line per inner array) help Print this message or the help of the given subcommand(s) ``` \## Usage Mount `./data` into the container. Text inputs strip lone `\\r` bytes (CRLF) but otherwise aren't normalized. \### Train Basic: ```bash docker run --rm -v ./data:/data bpe train \\ --algorithm basic \\ --vocab-size 512 \\ --input /data/train.txt \\ --output /data/model.json ``` Regex: ```bash docker run --rm -v ./data:/data bpe train \\ --algorithm regex \\ --vocab-size 512 \\ --input /data/train.txt \\ --output /data/model.json ``` \### Encode Input is split on `\\n`; output is a JSON array of token arrays: ```json \[\[tok, tok, ...], \[tok, ...], ...] ``` ```bash docker run --rm -v ./data:/data bpe encode \\ --model /data/model.json \\ --input /data/test.txt \\ --output /data/tokens.json ``` `tokenizer\_type` in the model file selects basic vs regex automatically. \### Decode ```bash docker run --rm -v ./data:/data bpe decode \\ --model /data/model.json \\ --input /data/tokens.json \\ --output /data/output.txt ``` Each inner token array becomes one output line, joined with `\\n`. \## Model JSON ```json { "tokenizer\_type": "basic", "vocab\_size": 512, "pattern": null, "special\_tokens": {}, "merges": \[\[101, 32], \[116, 32], ...] } ``` Regex models set `tokenizer\_type` to `"regex"` and include `pattern`. No extra fields. \## Validation Unit tests (reference parity): ```bash cargo test ``` Docker end-to-end (determinism + round-trip, basic + regex): ```bash chmod +x tests/submission\_val.sh tests/part2\_roundtrip.sh ./tests/submission\_val.sh ``` \### Part 2 (large file, 1 GB memory) Encode/decode stream line-by-line so multi‑hundred‑MB files stay within memory limits. With the assessment 150 MB corpus under `data/`: ```bash PART2\_CORPUS=./data/large\_corpus.txt PART2\_REPEATS=1 ./tests/part2\_roundtrip.sh ``` Without that file, the script builds a \~40 MB synthetic corpus from `train.txt` and runs round-trip under `docker run --memory=1g`. CI runs `cargo fmt`, `clippy`, `cargo test`, and the validation script on push/PR. \## Checking against reference data | File | Purpose | |------|---------| | `data/reference/basic\_512\_model.json` | Expected basic merges | | `data/reference/regex\_512\_model.json` | Expected regex merges + pattern | | `data/reference/basic\_512\_tokens.json` | Per-string encode expectations (basic) | | `data/reference/regex\_512\_tokens.json` | Per-string encode expectations (regex) | Train on `data/train.txt` with `--vocab-size 512` and compare merges. Encode `data/test.txt` line-by-line and compare tokens. \## Determinism Same input + vocab size + algorithm should give identical model JSON and token JSON across repeated runs. The validation script diffs duplicate train/encode outputs and checks `test.txt` round-trips. \## Dependencies \- `clap`, `serde`, `serde\_json` \- `fancy-regex` (GPT-4 split pattern needs lookahead) \## Docs \- `AGENTS.md` — agent instructions (assessment) \- `docs/PROCESS.md` , chronological dev notes \- `docs/PLAN.md`, `docs/minbpe\_notes.md` , earlier planning notes \## License MIT (see `Cargo.toml`).
